coredata.dc:title: Highly stable and selective measurement of Fe3+ ions under environmentally relevant conditions via an excitation-based multiwavelength method using N, S-doped carbon dots
coredata.dc:creator: Cheng, Hui-Yuan
Li, De-Chang
Cheng, Bin-Hai
Jiang, Hong
coredata.dc:description: Fast and accurate detection of Fe3+ under relevant natural conditions is important in environmental monitoring. In this study, an improved and simplified fluorescence method based on the multiwavelength luminescence in the visible region and the avoidance of the self-quenching property of N, S-doped carbon dots (NSC-Dots) was developed for the first time to determine Fe3+ concentration under varied environmental conditions. This method can simultaneously save time and provide accurate information. The as-prepared NSC-Dots exhibit two stable excitation peaks from 200 nm to 450 nm at a fixed emission wavelength (λ em = 450 nm). A standard equation (R 2 = 0.995) can be derived by measuring the quenching degree of the two peaks and referring to Stern–Volmer theory. Thus, Fe3+ concentration was accurately determined. The interference of the environmentally relevant concentrations of other metal ions, humic acid, and pH on Fe3+ measurement was tested. Results showed that the standard equation can be used to accurately determine Fe3+ concentration within the range of the 95% prediction band. The fast and facile multiwavelength method may facilitate the real-time monitoring of Fe3+ concentration in complex water environments.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fe3+ ions
Carbon dots
Fluorescence
Detection
Multiwavelength

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21677138 , 21876166 ), and the Key Special Program on the S&T for the Pollution Control, and Treatment of Water Bodies (No. 2017ZX07603-003 ). Appendix A

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a-b) TEM and HRTEM image of NSC-Dots. (c) XRD pattern, (d) Raman spectrum, and (e) FTIR spectrum of synthesized NSC-Dots.
(a) XPS spectra of the NSC-Dots and (b–e) high-resolution XPS spectra of the C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, and S 2p of the NSC-Dots, respectively.

(a) UV–vis absorption (black line) and fluorescence emission (blue line) spectra of the NSC-Dots. Insets show the corresponding photographs taken under visible light (left) and UV light of 370 nm (right). (b) Contour fluorescence intensity spectra of NSC-Dots. (c) Fluorescence decay curves of NSC-Dots (λ ex = 370 nm) measured at 450 nm.

Fluorescence intensity of NSC-Dots responses to variations in (a) pH (the control one without treatment after the preparation), (b) time, (c) UV light, and (d) heat treatment (RT means room temperature, which equal to 18 °C).
Fluorescence responses of NSC-Dots in the presence of different types of metal ions ([Mn+] = 500 μM; A represents all metal ions except Fe3+; B represents all metal ions, including Fe3+).

(a) Excitation spectra of NSC-Dots in the presence of various concentrations of Fe3+ (λ em = 450 nm), (b) Stern–Volmer plots for the NSC-Dots and Fe3+ system through the excitation and emission spectra data, and (c) fitting the modified Stern–Volmer equation for the NSC-Dots and Fe3+ system based on the excitation and emission spectra.
Modified Stern–Volmer plots for the NSC-Dots and Fe3+ under the influences of (a) different concentrations of Fe2+, (b) other common ions ([Mn+] = 500 μM) and humic acid (c = 10−3 M), and (c) pH.
Comparison between this work and reported literatures for Fe3+ detector.

head.ce:title: Highly stable and selective measurement of Fe3+ ions under environmentally relevant conditions via an excitation-based multiwavelength method using N, S-doped carbon dots

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: CAS Key Laboratory of Urban Pollutant Conversion, Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China, Hefei 230026, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: CAS Key Laboratory of Urban Pollutant Conversion, Department of Chemistry, University of Science and Technology of China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Fast and accurate detection of Fe3+ under relevant natural conditions is important in environmental monitoring. In this study, an improved and simplified fluorescence method based on the multiwavelength luminescence in the visible region and the avoidance of the self-quenching property of N, S-doped carbon dots (NSC-Dots) was developed for the first time to determine Fe3+ concentration under varied environmental conditions. This method can simultaneously save time and provide accurate information. The as-prepared NSC-Dots exhibit two stable excitation peaks from 200 nm to 450 nm at a fixed emission wavelength (λ em = 450 nm). A standard equation (R 2 = 0.995) can be derived by measuring the quenching degree of the two peaks and referring to Stern–Volmer theory. Thus, Fe3+ concentration was accurately determined. The interference of the environmentally relevant concentrations of other metal ions, humic acid, and pH on Fe3+ measurement was tested. Results showed that the standard equation can be used to accurately determine Fe3+ concentration within the range of the 95% prediction band. The fast and facile multiwavelength method may facilitate the real-time monitoring of Fe3+ concentration in complex water environments.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (Fe), as the fourth most abundant element in the Earth's crust (5.63%) (Gordon et al., 1982), is a major element in underground and surface waters and is recognized as a crucial substance in biogeochemical cycling (Aumont et al., 2003). Fe plays an indispensable role in many biological processes, such as hemoglobin formation, muscle and brain functions, and oxygen transport (Dang et al., 2012). Thus, either the deficiency or excess of Fe relative to normal limits can disturb cellular homeostasis and metabolism (Wen et al., 2016) and cause further disorder in an ecosystem. Moreover, Fe is commonly utilized in sewage treatment systems, such as zero valent Fe (Bakshi et al., 2018; Yoshino et al., 2018; Jiang et al., 2019), Fenton reagent (Peeters et al., 2015; Qiu et al., 2015), flocculation (Rabiet et al., 2014), and different kinds of hydrolytic Fe species can bring various effect on these environment progresses (Feng and Deng, 2000; Rai et al., 2018).

Considering the pervasive distribution of Fe in surface water and wastewater and its vital role in biogeochemical processes, the rapid and precise detection of Fe in water environments is crucial. Several methods have been developed for Fe ion quantification, such as ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) absorption spectroscopy (Horwell et al., 2003; Yan et al., 2017), electrochemical analysis (Göde et al., 2017), atomic absorption spectroscopy (AAS) (Barzegar et al., 2018; Chen et al., 2015), and inductively coupled plasma (ICP) (Graser et al., 2015; Rodríguez-Romero et al., 2016). However, each detection method has its own drawbacks. For example, UV–vis absorption spectroscopy always requires additional reagents or the fine synthesis of probe compounds (Yan et al., 2017), which is harmful to the environment. Electrochemical analysis is effective for Fe measurement, but causes the problems of interference and chemical fouling (Gumpu et al., 2015). Other measurement techniques, including AAS and ICP, require expensive equipment, are time-consuming, and must be performed by trained professionals (Tuan and Seung, 2010). Meanwhile, real water samples are frequently affected by numerous interference factors, such as pH variation and the coexistence of other metallic ions, which require arduous pretreatment before measurement. Compared with these approaches, fluorescence-based methods are promising in Fe ion detection due to their advantages of simple and easy operation, high sensitivity, and high selectivity (Quang and Kim, 2010).

The use of the quenching effect of fluorescent materials in detecting metal ions has recently elicited extensive attention (Lu et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2015a). Among these materials, carbon dots (C-Dots) have attracted considerable attention and are widely applied in many fields given their facile functionalization, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility (Atchudan et al., 2016; Sun et al., 2013). C-Dots can generate strong multicolor luminescence in the visible region (Ye et al., 2013). Some researchers have reported the use of C-Dots in detecting metal ions (Atchudan et al., 2017; Ding et al., 2014; Yao et al., 2018) or organic matters (Chao et al., 2018; Guan et al., 2017) based on their particular fluorescence property. Interestingly, doped heteroatoms can avoid self-quenching due to their substantial ensemble Stokes shift (Lu et al., 2015). Furthermore, several studies have indicated that surface groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxylate, and amino groups, can form effective coordination/chelation interactions with Fe3+ ions and further lead to the quenching effect of C-Dots (Li et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2015c). Fe3+ ions can be qualitatively and quantitatively detected using this character. However, these studies (Ding et al., 2014; Xu et al., 2015c) utilized only single-emission wavelengths in detection, which cannot provide sufficient spectral information for the aforementioned pollutants (Gong et al., 2012). Multiwavelength determination using 3D excitation–emission matrix (EEM) fluorescence spectroscopy can improve accuracy and reliability by presenting the changes in different wavelengths (Ziegmann et al., 2010). However, conventional EEM assembled from several emission spectra under different excitation wavelengths typically require considerable time to obtain high-quality spectra (Nahorniak and Booksh, 2006; Ziegmann et al., 2010). Moreover, the wavelength of a fluorescence peak always shifts with a change in the surrounding environment, which further causes inaccuracy in a detection system.

Given that many C-Dots are excitation independent and the wavelength of an emission peak does not shift with a change in wavelength excitation (D et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2013; Lu et al., 2015; Xu et al., 2015a; Yang et al., 2014a, 2014b), we proposed a fast and facile Fe3+ detection method based on N, S-doped C-Dots (NSC-Dots) that can simultaneously investigate the change in two excitation wavelengths by fixing the emission wavelength. This method can considerably reduce scanning time and simultaneously obtain sufficient information about the quenching process. Thereafter, we synthesized NSC-Dots, demonstrated selectivity and sensitivity in detecting Fe3+ by quenching the fluorescence intensity of NSC-Dots, obtained the standard equation for Fe3+ quantification based on Stern–Volmer theory, and validated the feasibility of this method under diverse environmental conditions.

All reagents were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Shanghai (China). The chemicals were of analytical grade and used without further purification. Deionized water was used throughout the experiments. Aqueous solutions of Fe2+, Fe3+, Na+, Zn2+, Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Ni2+, and Cu2+ were prepared from FeSO4·7H2O, Fe(NO3)3·9H2O, NaNO3, Zn(NO3)2·6H2O, Al(NO3)3·9H2O, Hg2Cl2, Pb(NO3)2, Cr(NO3)3·9H2O, BaCl2·2H2O, Ca(NO3)2·4H2O, Cd(NO3)2·4H2O, Co(NO3)2·6H2O, KNO3, Mg(NO3)2·6H2O, Mn(NO3)2·4H2O, Ni(NO3)2·6H2O, and Cu(NO3)2·2H2O, respectively.

In this study, 0.42 g (2.0 mmol) sodium citrate and 0.46 g (6.0 mmol) thiourea were mixed in an agate mortar and ground to obtain a uniform powder. The mixture was placed in a 160 mL zirconium material autoclave equipped with a heating furnace (Weihai Xinhui Chemical Machinery Co., Ltd.). The temperature was monitored and logged on a proportional–integral–derivative (PID) temperature controller, and pressure was determined using a piezometer. Preparation was performed at an initial H2 pressure of 2 MPa and 200 °C for 1 h. After the reaction, the autoclave was allowed to cool naturally to room temperature (25 °C), and the gas was released. Then, the product was dissolved in 40 mL deionized water and centrifuged at 8000 rpm for 30 min. After the centrifugation, the supernatant transparent solution was purified through dialyzing against pure water by a dialysis membrane for seven days for characterizations. In order to cut down the expense and save time, the supernatant was directly used in detection experiments.

Fluorescence measurements were performed on an F-4500 spectrofluorometer (Hitachi, Japan) equipped with a 1 cm × 1 cm quartz cell. The slit widths of excitation and emission were 10 nm and 5 nm, respectively. Fluorescence decay curves of NSC-Dots was conducted on Fluorolog-3 (HORIBA, US). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was conducted on an ESCALAB™ 250 X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (Thermo Scientific Inc., UK) with a monochromatized Al K α radiation (1486.9 eV). Surface charging effects were corrected with the C 1 s peak at 284.6 eV, and spectra processing was fitted using the XPSPEAK 4.1 software. Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) was conducted on an Equinox 55 IR spectrometer (Bruker, Germany) with a detection range of 4000–400 cm−1. Raman spectrum was recorded on Renishaw inVia, UK, equipped with a power a 532 nm He–Ne laser. Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) was performed on a JEOL-2100F transmission electron microscope (Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) analysis of NSC-Dots was performed on multifunctional target (TTR-III, Rigaku, Japan) with Cu Kα radiation (30 kV/160 mA, λ = 1.54056 Å) in a scan range of 10–90°.

NSC-Dots (2.0 mL 0.15 mg mL−1) were first added to a 1.0 cm quartz cell, and then 0.01 M metal ion solution was gradually titrated into the cell at room temperature. The resulting mixtures were used for luminescence measurements immediately after stirring.

Morphology and microstructure were identified using the TEM image of the prepared NSC-Dot solution. As shown in Fig. 1a, the NSC-Dots have a regular shape and are monodisperse. HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) shows NSC-Dots are absence of discernible lattice structures, which indicates that they are amorphous carbon. The XRD pattern of the NSC-Dots possess a broader peak around 25° (Fig. 1c), which represents the presence of highly disordered carbon atom (Zhu et al., 2013) and may attribute to the doped nitrogen-, sulfur-, and oxygen-containing groups, revealing an amorphous carbon phase (Sun et al., 2013). Raman spectrum (Fig. 1d) shows that NSC-Dots have two broad peak at 1335 and 1545 cm−1, which correspond to D-band (sp3 defects) and G-band (sp2 carbon), respectively. The coexistence of both bands further indicates that the NSC-Dots are amorphous (Liu et al., 2009; Qiao et al., 2010).

The functional groups of NSC-Dots were characterized via FTIR (Fig. 1e). The peaks at 1795 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1 can be ascribed to C=O bending vibrations and the COO− group, respectively (Xu et al., 2015c). The other bands at 3186 cm−1 and 1116 cm−1 represent the typical stretching modes of N–H and C–N, respectively. The presence of the vibrational peak at 619 cm−1 is associated with S–S stretching, that at 712 cm−1 is related to the C–S group, and that at 2516 cm−1 is attributed to the free sulfhydryl (–SH) (D et al., 2017). These results confirmed the doped state of N and S on NSC-Dots.

XPS was conducted to investigate the chemical structure of NSC-Dots. Fig. 2a presents the XPS spectra of NSC-Dots, which show four typical peaks: S 2p (~ 164 eV), C 1s (~ 285 eV), N 1s (~ 400 eV), and O 1s (~ 532 eV). In the high-resolution C 1 s XPS spectrum of NSC-Dots (Fig. 2b), three peaks are observed with binding energies of approximately 284.7, 285.5, and 288.0 eV, which are attributed to the C–C, C–S/C–N/C–O, and C=O species, respectively. The high-resolution spectra of N 1s (Fig. 2c) show the presence of pyridinic (399.2 eV) and pyrrolic (399.8 eV) types. The O 1s spectrum in Fig. 2d has two peaks at 531.5 and 533.1 eV, which are attributed to C=O and C–OH/–O–, respectively. The S 2p spectrum in Fig. 2e consists of two peaks centered at 163.7 and 168.5 eV, thereby suggesting that S exists in two forms. The former peak can be deconvoluted into two distinct components at 163.5 eV and 164.1 eV, which agree with the reported 2p 3/2 and 2p 1/2 positions of the C–S–C covalent bond of thiophene-S due to their spin–orbit couplings. The latter peak can be attributed to –C–SOx– (x = 2, 3, 4) species, such as sulfate or sulfonate (Sun et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2015c). Surface state exploration also demonstrated that the N and S atoms were successfully doped into NSC-Dots.

The possible formation mechanism of the NSC-Dots involves the processes of dehydration, polymerization, and carbonization (Atchudan et al., 2018; Edison et al., 2016; Hsu and Chang, 2012). Firstly, during the process of heating, hydroxyl groups would undergo dehydration. Then, the amino groups and sulfur atom in thiourea might react with carbonyl groups of sodium citrate that will form bigger sized polymers. The polymerization process would lead to a short single burst of nucleation and the resulting nuclei then grow by the diffusion of solutes toward the particle surfaces. Then, the above water soluble polymers would undergo carbonization and finally give NSC-Dots.

The remarkable optical properties of the as-obtained NSC-Dots were analyzed by UV–vis absorption. The UV–vis spectrum ( Fig. 3a) exhibits a sharp characteristic peak at 337 nm, which is ascribed to the n-π* transitions of the carbonyl (D et al., 2017). Moreover, a small hump at ~220 nm implies the π-π* transition of aromatic or nitrogen heterocyclic sp2 domain (D et al., 2017; Eda et al., 2010). The narrow and symmetrical fluorescence peak at 450 nm, resulted from their narrow size distribution, was observed when the aqueous solution was excited at 370 nm (Xu et al., 2015d). The digital picture of the aqueous dispersions further shows that the obtained C-Dots are water-soluble and exhibit very bright blue luminescence under 370 nm UV light even at the low concentration of 0.15 mg mL−1.

The optical property of NSC-Dots was also characterized via EEM fluorescence spectroscopy. Fig. 3b shows that the contour fluorescence intensity of NSC-Dots exhibits two peaks at λ ex = 265 nm and 370 nm with the nearly fixed λ em = 450 nm. Notably, the fixed emission wavelength (λ em) of the peaks demonstrates that the as-prepared NSC-Dots exhibit the independence of the emission spectra within the range of λ ex from 240 nm to 400 nm. Moreover, the excitation spectra can simultaneously provide the fluorescence information of the two peaks (λ ex = 265 nm and 370 nm) and additional information for detecting Fe ions. The excitation-independent behavior has been previously reported (D et al., 2017; Dong et al., 2013; Yang et al., 2014a, 2014b). Dong et al. presumed the doped N and S in C-Dots may exhibit a synergy effect, suppress the original O-state in C-Dots, and enhance the N-state; thus, NSC-Dots exhibit high excitation-independent fluorescence (Dong et al., 2013). The fluorescence lifetime of NSC-Dots was measured and the value was calculated to be 5.53 ± 0.34 ns according to single exponential decay kinetics. Such a short lifetime indicated that the radiative recombination of excitations gives rise to fluorescence (Yang et al., 2014a, 2014b).

To evaluate the stability of NSC-dots, fluorescence intensity of NSC-Dots were tested under different conditions. The pH of NSC-Dots was 8.2. Fig. 4a shows the effects of pH on the intensity of the fluorescence spectra over a wide range of pH (3.0–12.0). NSC-Dots exhibit negligible change in the region of pH 3.0–10.0, thereby suggesting that NSC-Dots are stable and insensitive over this wide range of pH and can work under approximate environmental conditions. However, NSC-Dots demonstrate slightly poor stability and are quenched at pH levels 11 and 12. Such phenomenon may be attributed to the change in surface charge of hydroxyl and carboxylic due to deprotonation (Dong et al., 2013). Notably, the fluorescence intensity of NSC-Dots (Fig. 4b) nearly remained similar within 10 months of storage at room temperature, thereby indicating that NSC-Dots exhibit good stability for a long period, which is critical to practical applications. Moreover, the fluorescent stability under the UV illumination and at different temperature was investigated. As showed in Fig. 4c, the fluorescence intensity of both peaks showed no obvious decrease within 24 h under the illumination. Besides, the fluorescence intension declined a little at the different temperature with the range from 0 to 60 °C (Fig. 4d). These results indicated that NSC-Dots have good fluorescent stability under different physiological environments.

To test the potential application of NSC-Dots as a fluorescent probe, fluorescence responses were recorded in the presence of common metal ions at a concentration of 500 μM. Fig. 5 shows that the fluorescence intensities of two peaks (peak A, λ ex = 265 nm and peak B, λ ex = 370 nm) vary in the presence of different metal ions. Most ions, such as Na+, Zn2+, Al3+, Hg+, Cr3+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, K+, Mg2+, Mn2+, and Cu2+, cause no apparent change in the intensity of NSC-Dots, in which the values of F/F 0 (F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of a quencher, respectively) are 95.1–103.9% and 92.5–99.4% for peaks A and B, respectively, thereby indicating that these metal ions have no quenching effect on the fluorescence property of NSC-Dots. Pb2+, Co2+, Ni2+, and Fe2+ slightly influence the intensity of NSC-Dots, with F/F 0 being in the range of 77.9–91.1% and 79.4–88.5% for peaks A and B, respectively. Exceptionally, peaks A of Hg2+ and Cu2+ are largely quenched to 60.8% and 52.8%, respectively, which indicates that Hg2+ and Cu2+ have a significant quenching effect on the fluorescence property of NSC-Dots. However, peaks B of Hg2+ and Cu2+ are only quenched to 83.9% and 71.3%, respectively.

Compared with other metal ions, Fe3+ can considerably quench the intensity of both peaks to 28.9% and 38.4% simultaneously. Moreover, although peak A is quenched to 32.9% when all the other metal ions are present in the solution, the fluorescence intensity of peak B remains at 73.3%. By adding the ion of Fe3+ to the aforementioned system, the fluorescence intensities of both peaks are quenched to 10.8% and 44.0%, respectively. Among all the studied metal ions, Fe3+ can simultaneously and drastically quench peaks A and B of NSC-Dots. On the basis of the distinct quenching property of Fe3+ toward NSC-Dot fluorescence, NSC-Dots are highly selective to Fe3+, whereas other metal ions cannot exert a substantial quenching effect on both peaks.

The outstanding selectivity and specificity can be ascribed to the abundant hydroxyl, carboxylate, and amino groups of NSC-Dots, which can form effective coordination/chelation interactions with Fe3+ ions and lead to nonradiative electron transfer or energy transfer (Li et al., 2013; Qu et al., 2013; Xu et al., 2015c). Therefore, the quenching effect can be caused by Fe3+ ions. Moreover, the low electronegativity of the S heteroatom can tune the electronic local density of NSC-Dots. Thus, nearby O atoms tend to have a higher surface electron density and further form coordination bonds with Fe3+, through which the fluorescence intensity of NSC-Dots are quenched (Li et al., 2014).

The quenching property of Fe3+ toward NSC-Dot fluorescence can be used to qualitatively detect Fe3+ in solutions. To further quantify the relationship between Fe3+ concentration and the fluorescence intensity of NSC-Dots, the fluorescence spectra of NSC-Dots were obtained upon titration with Fe3+. Fig. 6a presents the quenching effect of Fe3+ ions on the fluorescence of NSC-Dots. Fluorescence intensity decreases gradually with increasing concentration of Fe3+ ions. The fluorescence quenching data were analyzed using the Stern–Volmer equation as follows: (Zhang et al., 2009)
(1)
F
0
F
= 1 +
k
q
τ
0
[ Q ] = 1 +
K
sv
[ Q ]
where F 0 and F are the fluorescence intensities in the absence and presence of different concentrations of quenchers, respectively; K sv is the Stern–Volmer quenching constant; [Q] is the quencher concentration; k q is the quenching rate constant of the biological macromolecule; and k q = K sv/τ 0.τ 0(10−8 s) is the average lifetime of a molecule without any quencher.

The fitted plot shown in Fig. 6b presents a good linear relationship within the range of 0–500 μM. Moreover, the values of k q are considerably greater than the maximum diffusion collision constant (2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1) (Ware, 1962), which indicates that the quenching mechanism between Fe3+ and NSC-Dots is static quenching. The limit of detection (LOD) for Fe3+ ions at peaks A and B are 0.14 μM and 0.15 μM (S/N = 3), respectively, which are lower than the maximum level of Fe3+ ions (ca. 5.4 μM) permitted in drinking water by the World Health Organization (WHO, 2011). Compared with previous studies ( Table 1), the probe NSC-Dots have a large detection range and a low detection limit, thereby indicating the applicability of an NSC-Dot sensor for Fe3+.

The conventional mono-wavelength method was performed to verify the accuracy of the proposed multiwavelength method. Given λ ex = 265 nm and λ ex = 370 nm, the emission spectra of NSC-Dots in the presence of different Fe3+ concentrations were recorded (Fig. S1). The quenching values were then correlated to the Fe3+ concentrations (Fig. 6b and Table S1). The results showed that the standard curves (dashed curves in Fig. 6b) obtained from the mono-wavelength method coincide with those obtained from the multiwavelength method.

To facilitate application, the changes in fluorescence intensity of the two peaks in the presence and absence of Fe3+ were combined into a Stern–Volmer equation, and a modified standard curve was obtained as follows (Eq. (2)):
(2)
F
0
A
F
A
+
F
0
B
F
B
=
b
+
K
[
Q
]
where subscripts A and B represent peaks A and B, respectively; and b and K are the intercept and slope, respectively. The fitting plot in Fig. 6c presents a good relationship between the fluorescence intensity of peaks A and B and Fe3+ concentration. Similarly, the modified Stern–Volmer standard curve based on the mono-wavelength method can be obtained (Table S2), and a slight difference is observed between the two curves, thereby indicating the accuracy of the multiwavelength method.

Table S3 compares the detection time of different methods. The multiwavelength method can simultaneously provide the fluorescence information of two peaks, whereas the mono-wavelength method can show only one peak in a spectrum, and thus, it requires more time and energy to obtain two spectra. EEM fluorescence spectroscopy can provide comprehensive information about the two peaks, but it wastes considerable time in scanning. Therefore, the excitation-based method is faster and more convenient than the other fluorescence methods.

The coexistence of multitudinous compounds in natural environments poses a considerable challenge to detection methods not only in terms of sensitivity but also selectivity. Thus, the interference of other metal ions and humic acid to Fe3+ detection was determined. The influence of Fe2+ was first investigated because Fe2+ always coexists with Fe3+. As shown in Fig. 7a, the fitting-modified Stern–Volmer plots exhibit high linear correlation under different concentrations of Fe2+, and all data points are within the 95% prediction band, which indicates that different concentrations of Fe2+ hardly influence Fe3+ detection. Therefore, the obtained standard curve can be utilized in detecting Fe3+ under the influence of Fe2+.

The influences of other common metal ions, such as Mg2+, Ca2+, K+, and Na+, were also investigated. Fig. 7b presents the modified Stern–Volmer plots of NSC-Dots under the influences of different common ions. The modified Stern–Volmer plots under the interference of different other ions are all within the range of the 95% prediction band, thereby indicating that these ions exert negligible interference on Fe3+ detection. In addition, the most common organic matter, i.e., humic acid, slightly influences Fe3+ detection. These results suggest the fitting curve of this method can be utilized in measuring Fe3+ under the influence of other common ions and humic acid.

The effects of pH on the sensitivity and selectivity of the multiwavelength method in detecting Fe3+ was investigated. As shown in Fig. 7c, a good linear correlation is exhibited under pH < 9.0. The data points under pH = 5.0–8.0 are nearly within the range of the 95% prediction band, although a few points of high Fe3+ concentrations are found at the border of the prediction band. The result clearly indicates that the probe NSC-Dots can be used within a broad range of pH (5.0–8.0). These results further imply that the NSC-Dots can meet the detection requirements for practical use of the multiwavelength method and the simplified standard curve under complex water environmental conditions.

NSC-Dots were successfully synthesized and exhibited excitation-independent behavior, in which two peaks had the same emission wavelength (λ em = 450 nm). A method based on the fluorescence excitation spectrum of NSC-Dots was developed for detecting Fe3+ by utilizing the two peaks. This method can save time and provide accurate information. The modified Stern–Volmer equation can fit (R 2 = 0.995) the change in fluorescence intensity well at different Fe3+ concentrations and can be used to quantify Fe3+ concentration. The obtained standard equation can be used to accurately determine Fe3+ concentration under environmentally relevant conditions within the range of the 95% prediction band.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge financial support from National Natural Science Foundation of China (21677138, 21876166), and the Key Special Program on the S&T for the Pollution Control, and Treatment of Water Bodies (No. 2017ZX07603-003).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.envres.2018.12.023

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Hydrothermal conversion of Magnolia liliiflora into nitrogen-doped carbon dots as an effective turn-off fluorescence sensing, multi-colour cell imaging and fluorescent ink
Facile green synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots using Chionanthus retusus fruit extract and investigation of their suitability for metal ion sensing and biological application
Nitrogen-doped carbon dots originating from unripe peach for fluorescent bioimaging and electrocatalytic oxygen reduction reaction
An ecosystem model of the global ocean including Fe, Si, P colimitations
Arsenic sorption on zero-valent iron-biochar complexes
4-Chlorophenol degradation using ultrasound/peroxymonosulfate/nanoscale zero valent iron: reusability, identification of degradation intermediates and potential application for real wastewater
Solvent-dependent carbon dots and their applications in detection of water in organic solvents
Redox speciation analysis of dissolved iron in estuarine and coastal waters with on-line solid phase extraction and graphite furnace atomic absorption spectrometry detection
A “Turn-On” thiol functionalized fluorescent carbon quantum dot based chemosensory system for arsenite detection
